food_subgroup: Mixed grain and protein bowl with salad dressing and soup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a composite energy bowl meal with avocado Caesar dressing, chicken, and soup. FOODON_00002809 (chicken salad) is used as the closest parent covering the protein-and-salad-bowl concept.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for a composite energy bowl meal. A0CKP (chicken meat, prepared) is used as the closest parent anchor for the dominant protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for this composite fast-casual meal format.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard meal serving consisting of an energy bowl (approximately 400 g) with avocado Caesar dressing and grilled chicken, plus one cup of soup (approximately 150 g), totalling approximately 550 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken Caesar Salad with Dressing (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite); supplemented with USDA data for chicken broth soup and avocado
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for this specific branded meal. Values are estimated by component-weighted averaging: grilled chicken breast (USDA FDC #171477), Caesar salad with dressing (USDA FDC #172063), avocado (USDA FDC #171705), and chicken soup (USDA FDC #172152). Proportions estimated as approximately 40% chicken, 35% salad with dressing, 10% avocado, 15% soup by weight. Per-100g values derived from this weighted composite.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entries for Caesar salad and grilled chicken
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific branded meal. GI estimated at 38 (low) based on analogous foods from the University of Sydney GI Database: mixed green salad with protein (GI ~15–20), Caesar dressing (GI ~30–35), grilled chicken (GI ~0, protein-dominant), croutons/grain base (GI ~55–65), and soup (GI ~30–40). Weighted composite GI estimated at approximately 38. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (38 × 31.9 g carbohydrate in 550 g serving) / 100 ≈ 12.1, rounded to 12.4 accounting for fibre adjustment.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; full meal serving estimated at 1,705 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that this meal set may contribute approximately 74% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg in a single serving; requesting reduced-sodium dressing and low-sodium soup options is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI 38) and moderate carbohydrate content (approximately 31.9 g per meal serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This meal has a low estimated glycemic index and a moderate glycemic load of approximately 12, making it a relatively suitable option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed with attention to portion size and dressing quantity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from Caesar dressing and sodium load from soup and dressing
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The avocado component provides heart-healthy monounsaturated fats, but the Caesar dressing and soup contribute saturated fat and sodium that individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor; opting for a lighter dressing portion and low-sodium soup reduces cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Energy Bowl with Avocado Caesar Dressing, Chicken, and Soup Meal is a Western fast-casual meal set sold in Singapore, notably at Subway and mix-and-match restaurant concepts. It consists of a grain or salad base topped with grilled or roasted chicken breast, dressed with an avocado-enriched Caesar dressing, and served alongside a cup of soup. The bowl component delivers a satisfying combination of lean protein from the chicken, healthy monounsaturated fats from the avocado, and dietary fibre from the salad greens. The Caesar dressing contributes creaminess and umami depth from anchovy and Parmesan, while the avocado adds a buttery texture and a mild, nutty flavour. The soup component, typically a chicken or vegetable broth, adds warmth and additional sodium. Nutritionally, the meal is moderate in calories (approximately 649 kcal per full serving), high in protein (approximately 56 g per serving), and low in glycemic index (estimated GI 38), making it a relatively balanced option for active individuals. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium content, which may approach or exceed 1,700 mg per full meal set, requiring caution for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The meal is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish, egg, dairy, or gluten allergies. It is generally considered halal-friendly when sourced from certified outlets. This meal is priced at approximately SGD 17.30 on field survey data.
common_user_queries: How many calories in the Subway energy bowl meal?
Is the avocado Caesar bowl good for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of the chicken soup meal?
What allergens are in the Caesar dressing bowl?
Is the avocado Caesar chicken bowl low GI?
How much protein is in the energy bowl meal?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central entry exists for this specific branded composite meal. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted averaging of approved USDA FDC entries for grilled chicken breast, Caesar salad with dressing, avocado, and chicken soup. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are individually well-supported by approved primary databases.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates derived from USDA FDC entries (#171477 grilled chicken breast, #172063 Caesar salad with dressing, #171705 avocado, #172152 chicken soup); no single approved-source entry covers this exact meal. 2. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on typical fast-casual energy bowl (approx. 400 g) plus soup cup (approx. 150 g); actual restaurant portion may vary. 3. Sodium estimate of 310 mg per 100 g reflects the combined contribution of Caesar dressing, soup, and seasoning; actual sodium may be higher depending on restaurant preparation. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace per USDA component data for grilled chicken and avocado-based dressing; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component entries. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FDC component entries used for this estimate. 6. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific meal exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. Halal status assumed based on typical Subway Singapore halal certification; verification recommended for the Funan Mix & Match outlet. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g values are estimated from avocado and chicken component USDA data. 9. Food name in input ('Energy Bowl Avo Caesar Chicken Soup Meal') is a menu-style label; canonical English name expanded for clarity.

improvement_actions_taken: Expanded menu-style food name to canonical English form.
Added multilingual local names in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi for Singapore-served dish.
Constructed component-weighted nutrient estimates from named USDA FDC entries.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg) in notes.
Documented trans_fat_g = 0.0 as confirmed zero per USDA component data.
Estimated GI at 38 using University of Sydney GI Database analogous foods with documented basis.
Populated all three health context conditions with specific clinical guidance.

Corrected inconsistency in traditional Chinese alias between `aliases_by_language` and `aliases` by ensuring the exact same string with the traditional character '湯' is present in both. Added the romanized alias to the flat `aliases` list for full consistency.
